followups.a: ZORDER rewrites files so rows with related values of the ZORDER columns are co-located in the same files, and Delta records the min/max statistics for those columns in the transaction log per file. Synapse serverless SQL reads the Delta transaction log and uses those file-level statistics to skip files where the min/max range doesn't overlap the query predicate — this is data skipping. Without ZORDER, rows are randomly distributed across files and the min/max ranges overlap heavily, so no files are skipped.

q: A Windows Server host running the self-hosted ADF Integration Runtime is upgraded from Server 2016 to Server 2022. What are the Service Principals, ARM template, and Performance Tuning considerations for a zero-downtime transition?
a: Zero-downtime transition: 1) Register a second Windows Server 2022 host to the same SHIR registration key — the SHIR supports up to 4 nodes and load-balances automatically. With two nodes, the 2016 host can be decommissioned without pipeline downtime. 2) Service Principals / Entra ID: the SHIR service account (MSA or SP) doesn't change — just re-install the SHIR on the 2022 host using the same registration key; the SP permissions to ADLS Gen2 and Azure SQL carry over. 3) ARM templates: if the SHIR host VM is managed via Bicep/ARM, update the VM SKU and OS image reference in the template. Use a new VM resource name (immutable infrastructure) so ARM creates the new VM alongside the old rather than modifying in place. 4) Performance Tuning: Windows Server 2022 supports TLS 1.3 and improved TCP stack; verify ADF data movement picks up the performance gain by running a benchmark copy before draining the old node.
fs: Add 2022 node with same key → SHIR load-balances → decom 2016; same SP/MSA; new VM name in ARM (immutable); benchmark TLS 1.3 throughput gain.
fj: Unregistering the old SHIR node before the new one is healthy — any in-flight ADF pipeline activities on the old node fail with no fallback.

followups.q: After the Windows Server 2022 upgrade, some SHIR-dependent pipelines error with 'cannot connect to the gateway'. What do you check first?
followups.a: Check that the SHIR service is running on the new node and is in the 'Online' state in the ADF monitoring panel. Verify the Windows Server 2022 firewall allows outbound HTTPS (port 443) to Azure Service Bus and the ADF control plane endpoints — Server 2022's enhanced security baseline may block endpoints that 2016 allowed. Check that the Service Principal or MSA used by the SHIR service has the same ADLS Gen2 and Azure SQL permissions as before, since the new host has a fresh Windows identity context.

q: Your data platform team uses Azure Synapse Analytics, Azure Data Factory pipelines, and Power Query Dataflows. How do you integrate a Purview business glossary term lifecycle with CI/CD so new terms are proposed, reviewed, and published via pull request rather than ad-hoc portal edits?
a: Export the business glossary from Purview as JSON via the Purview REST API (GET /catalog/api/atlas/v2/glossary) and commit it to git as the source of truth. New term proposals are submitted as pull requests modifying the glossary JSON — the PR review replaces the Purview governance workflow. On merge to main, a CI/CD pipeline calls the Purview REST API to create or update the term and map it to the relevant Azure Synapse Analytics tables and ADF pipeline outputs. Tag term updates with the pipeline run ID so Purview's audit log links each term version to a specific deployment. This makes the business glossary auditable, diff-able, and reproducible — the same properties you expect from Azure Data Factory ARM templates and Power Query M scripts.
fs: Glossary-as-code: export Purview business glossary to git JSON; PR for term proposals; CI/CD pipeline calls Purview API on merge; term versions linked to pipeline run ID.
fj: Editing business glossary terms directly in the Purview portal — changes are instant and untraceable; no review process, no rollback, no diff.

followups.q: The Purview REST API for glossary terms supports upsert. How do you handle term renames without breaking existing asset-to-term mappings?
followups.a: Purview terms are keyed by GUID, not by name — a rename updates the term's name but keeps the same GUID, so all asset mappings (to Azure Synapse Analytics, ADLS Gen2 paths, Azure SQL columns) remain intact. In your glossary JSON, track both the GUID and the current name. When generating the CI/CD upsert call, use the GUID as the key for PATCH requests so the pipeline updates the existing term rather than creating a duplicate.

q: You're migrating an on-prem SQL Server reporting workload to Azure SQL Managed Instance. Which Query Optimization steps do you perform before cutover to prevent a performance regression?
a: Run Database Experimentation Assistant (DEA) against the on-prem SQL Server workload to capture a trace, then replay it on Managed Instance in Test mode and compare query plan deltas. Enable Query Store on both source and target; export the forced-plan set from the source and import with `sp_query_store_force_plan` so well-known regressions can't appear after cutover. Validate columnstore index build times on MI's Gen5 hardware, since I/O characteristics differ from on-prem SAN. Use Automatic Plan Correction (ALTER DATABASE … SET AUTOMATIC_TUNING (FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN = ON)) on Managed Instance so the engine auto-reverts regressions post-migration. Run the workload for at least 24 hours in shadow mode using Query Store before promoting.
fs: DEA replay → import forced plans → enable FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN → shadow 24 h then cut over.
fj: Skipping DEA and just cutting over, assuming Query Optimization is 'good enough' on MI because it's SQL Server-compatible.

followups.q: After cutover to Managed Instance, Query Store shows a plan regression on a key ETL query that wasn't caught during shadow testing. What's your fastest fix?
followups.a: Force the last known good plan via `sys.sp_query_store_force_plan` using the plan_id from Query Store history. This is a live operation that takes effect on the next execution without a restart. Then investigate the new bad plan's context — parameter sniffing, stale statistics, or a cardinality estimator difference — and fix the root cause before releasing the forced plan.

followups.q: How does Managed Instance differ from Azure SQL Database for Query Optimization tuning options?
followups.a: Managed Instance exposes instance-level settings like `sp_configure` options (MAXDOP, cost threshold for parallelism, max server memory), trace flags at startup, and the SQL Server Agent — giving you much closer parity with on-prem SQL Server tuning. Azure SQL Database is a PaaS database where those instance-level knobs are either absent or managed by Microsoft, so you're limited to database-scoped configs and hints.

q: Your AKS-hosted data microservices are experiencing pod evictions during peak Spark job submission. How do you configure AKS node pools and Kubernetes resource quotas to prevent evictions from starving data workloads?
a: Separate workloads with dedicated node pools: a system node pool for kube-system components (tainted so application pods don't land there), a spot/preemptible node pool for Spark executors with tolerations for eviction, and an on-demand node pool for Spark drivers and critical microservices that must not be evicted. Set `requests` and `limits` on every pod — missing `requests` means the scheduler can over-commit nodes and trigger evictions. Apply ResourceQuotas per namespace to cap total CPU/memory any team can claim, and use LimitRange to set per-pod defaults. Add PodDisruptionBudgets for stateful microservices so voluntary disruptions (node drain for AKS upgrades) don't drop below minimum replicas. Use Cluster Autoscaler with scale-down delay tuned to Spark job durations so nodes aren't reclaimed mid-job.
fs: Separate node pools by criticality; set pod requests/limits; ResourceQuota + LimitRange; PDB for stateful pods; Cluster Autoscaler with job-aware scale-down delay.
fj: Mixing Spark executors and critical microservices on the same AKS node pool — Spark's burst CPU/memory triggers evictions that kill production services.

followups.q: Spark on AKS uses dynamic allocation but node scale-up is slow, delaying jobs. What AKS feature helps?
followups.a: Enable the AKS Virtual Nodes add-on (backed by Azure Container Instances) so Spark executors can burst onto serverless ACI pods with near-instant provisioning while the Cluster Autoscaler adds real VM nodes in the background. Alternatively, pre-provision a small warm pool using the AKS node pool `--min-count` and set a fast `scale-down-delay-after-add` so the pool doesn't immediately drain between jobs.

followups.q: How do you give Spark on AKS access to ADLS Gen2 without storing storage account keys in Kubernetes secrets?
followups.a: Use AKS Workload Identity: assign an Azure Managed Identity to the Spark service account via federated credential, then grant the identity 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' on the ADLS Gen2 account. The Spark pods exchange a projected service account token for an Azure AD token at runtime — no secrets in Kubernetes, no key rotation, and the identity is audited in Entra ID.

q: You need to enforce Query Optimization guardrails across 50 Azure SQL Database instances using PowerShell. What does a production-grade script look like?
a: Use the Az.Sql PowerShell module in a pipeline that iterates subscription→resource group→server→database. For each database call `Set-AzSqlDatabaseAdvisor` to turn on automatic index/statistics recommendations and `Set-AzSqlDatabase -AutomaticTuning` to enable CREATE_INDEX, DROP_INDEX, FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN. Export a before/after snapshot of each database's automatic tuning state to a CSV for the audit trail. Run the PowerShell script from an Azure Automation runbook on a weekly schedule so new databases added after initial deployment are caught. Gate destructive changes (DROP_INDEX auto-apply) behind a manual approval step in the runbook using the `Checkpoint-Workflow` pattern. Log to Log Analytics via the Az.OperationalInsights PowerShell cmdlets.
fs: Az.Sql module loop → Set-AzSqlDatabaseAdvisor + Set-AzSqlDatabase AutomaticTuning → Automation runbook weekly → Log Analytics audit.
fj: Clicking through the Azure portal per database — no auditability, no consistency, and it doesn't scale to 50+ instances.

code.label: Enable AutoTuning across all databases in a subscription
lines: # PowerShell equivalent pseudocode shown as comment blocks

followups.q: How do you detect which databases already have Automatic Tuning disabled so the script is idempotent?
followups.a: Call `Get-AzSqlDatabaseAdvisor` and check each advisor's `AutoExecuteStatus`. Only emit Set calls for databases where the current state differs from the desired state. Store the desired-state configuration in a JSON config file in source control so the script is declarative — it converges every run without re-applying already-correct settings.

q: An Azure Event Hubs consumer group is lagging. You need to attribute the lag to a specific partition and consumer using PowerShell without writing custom code. How?
a: Use the Az.EventHub PowerShell module: `Get-AzEventHubConsumerGroup` lists groups, then invoke the Event Hubs REST API via `Invoke-RestMethod` with an Entra-ID bearer token to call `/{namespace}/eventhubs/{name}/consumergroups/{group}/partitions/{id}` and inspect `lastEnqueuedSequenceNumber` vs the consumer's `lastSequenceNumber`. For a bulk view, export the per-partition offset snapshots to a CSV and compare enqueue vs consumer offsets to pinpoint the lagging partition. Alternatively, use Azure Monitor PowerShell cmdlets (`Get-AzMetric`) to pull `IncomingMessages`, `OutgoingMessages`, and `ActiveConnections` per partition from Azure Event Hubs metrics. Pair with a Log Analytics workspace query via `Invoke-AzOperationalInsightsQuery` to correlate with consumer application logs.
fs: Az.EventHub + REST API call per partition for sequence-number delta; Get-AzMetric for IncomingMessages vs OutgoingMessages; Log Analytics query for consumer logs.
fj: Jumping straight to scaling the consumer app — the lag may be on a single hot partition that needs repartitioning at the Azure Event Hubs namespace level, not more consumer replicas.

followups.q: The lagging partition turns out to be a hot partition caused by a skewed partition key. How do you fix it in Azure Event Hubs without losing messages?
followups.a: Azure Event Hubs partition count is fixed at namespace creation for Standard tier (changeable only at creation or by migrating to Premium/Dedicated). Short-term: change the producer's partition key strategy to distribute load (e.g., add a random suffix or use round-robin null key assignment). Long-term: create a new Event Hubs namespace with more partitions, run dual-write from producers during a drain window, then migrate consumers after old messages expire.

q: Design an Azure SQL Server Always On Availability Group on AKS using the SQL Server container image and Azure shared disks. What are the failure modes and how do you handle them?
a: Deploy three SQL Server pods in a StatefulSet with anti-affinity rules across AKS availability zones. Mount Azure shared managed disks (ReadWriteMany via Azure Disk CSI with MaxShares) for the quorum witness and use Azure shared NFS for the data directory. The primary pod advertises via a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service; replicas use a read-scale Service. Failure modes: pod eviction — Kubernetes restarts the pod on the same node with the persistent volume claim re-attached; the AG listener IP stays on the LoadBalancer Service so clients reconnect. Node failure — SQL Server AG failover promotes a synchronous replica; AKS replaces the node if Cluster Autoscaler is enabled. Zone outage — with synchronous replicas in two zones and an asynchronous replica in the third, you lose at most the async data; the two synchronous replicas form quorum. Use SQL Server PowerShell cmdlets (`Invoke-Sqlcmd`, `Add-SqlAvailabilityGroupListenerStaticIp`) in an init container to bootstrap the AG on first deployment.
fs: StatefulSet + anti-affinity across zones; shared disk for witness; LoadBalancer Service as AG listener; sync replicas in 2 zones; PowerShell init container for AG bootstrap.
fj: Using a single AKS node pool and hostPath storage for SQL Server — no zone redundancy, no persistent volume, data lost on node recycle.

followups.q: How do you perform a rolling SQL Server version upgrade on AKS without AG downtime?
followups.a: Use the SQL Server Operator pattern: the operator orchestrates upgrading secondary replicas first (one at a time), then performs a planned failover to a healthy upgraded secondary, then upgrades the old primary. This keeps the AG in a quorum-safe state throughout. Automate health checks between steps using `Invoke-Sqlcmd` health probes in PowerShell so the operator only proceeds when replica synchronization health is HEALTHY.

q: A Power BI report is slow against Azure SQL Server. Walk through a Query Optimization investigation from the Power BI side to the database engine.
a: Start in Power BI Performance Analyzer to capture DAX query timings and export the DAX queries. Use DAX Studio to run the offending query and capture SQL statements sent to Azure SQL Server via DirectQuery. In Azure SQL Server, open Query Store and look for the high-duration plan using sys.query_store_query_text. Examine the execution plan for key lookups (missing column in index), parameter sniffing (plan cached for an atypical parameter), or implicit type conversions invalidating indexes. Add a covering index for the lookup columns and validate with `SET STATISTICS IO ON`. If parameter sniffing is the root cause, use OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN or a Query Store forced plan. If Power BI is sending N+1 queries for slicers, rewrite the report's table relationships to reduce query fan-out at the DirectQuery layer.
fs: Performance Analyzer → DAX Studio capture SQL → Query Store plan analysis → covering index or forced plan.
fj: Throwing a higher Azure SQL Server tier at the problem without query-level diagnosis — the bottleneck is almost always plan quality or missing indexes, not raw compute.

followups.q: Query Store shows that Query Optimization auto-apply added an index that made the BI query faster but slowed a nightly write-heavy ETL. How do you handle the conflict?
followups.a: Automatic Tuning on Azure SQL Server applies changes database-wide and doesn't distinguish read from write workloads. Disable auto-apply for DROP/CREATE_INDEX and instead run the index recommendation manually after validating the trade-off. Use Query Store to capture both workloads' plan regressions. Long-term, partition the workload: either separate read and write databases (CQRS pattern) or schedule index rebuilds outside the ETL window.

q: Explain how you would implement Lakehouse Federation in Azure Databricks Unity Catalog to query a Snowflake table alongside Delta Lake tables without copying data.
a: Lakehouse Federation in Unity Catalog lets you register external query engines as foreign catalogs so Unity Catalog users query them with standard SQL without moving data. Steps: (1) Create a connection object in Unity Catalog (`CREATE CONNECTION snowflake_prod TYPE SNOWFLAKE`) with credentials stored in Unity Catalog secrets, not hardcoded. (2) Create a foreign catalog (`CREATE FOREIGN CATALOG sf_catalog USING CONNECTION snowflake_prod OPTIONS (database 'PROD')`) — this maps Snowflake's PROD database into Unity Catalog. (3) Query with a three-part name: `SELECT * FROM sf_catalog.schema.table JOIN main.delta_schema.fact USING (id)`. The Databricks query engine pushes predicate and projection pushdown to Snowflake via JDBC and brings back only the filtered result set. Permissions are enforced by Unity Catalog column masking and row filters; Snowflake credentials are never exposed to end users.
fs: CREATE CONNECTION → CREATE FOREIGN CATALOG → three-part SQL join; predicates pushed to source; Unity Catalog enforces masking on both sides.
fj: Copying the Snowflake table to ADLS Gen2 daily and joining — defeats the point of Lakehouse Federation, creates a stale copy, and doubles storage costs.

followups.q: How does Lakehouse Federation handle Unity Catalog row-level security when the foreign table is in Snowflake?
followups.a: Unity Catalog applies its own row filters on top of the data returned from Snowflake before the result reaches the user — it doesn't push RLS predicates into Snowflake's own security model. This means you get consistent Unity Catalog governance, but Snowflake's native row access policies are bypassed when accessed via Federation. Keep this in mind for compliance: the connection credential must be a Snowflake service account with read-only access to the specific objects, and Unity Catalog is the governance enforcement layer.

followups.q: Lakehouse Federation queries to Snowflake are slow. What optimizations are available?
followups.a: Lakehouse Federation pushes SQL to the foreign source, so first check whether predicate and projection pushdown is working by examining the query plan for `RemoteScan` with pushed predicates. Reduce JOIN complexity by pre-aggregating on the Snowflake side using a virtual view registered in the foreign catalog. If latency is consistently high, consider materializing hot Snowflake tables as Delta Lake tables via a scheduled Databricks job and using Lakehouse Federation only for low-frequency ad-hoc queries.

q: Your Azure Event Hubs namespace is at Standard tier and you're approaching the 20-consumer-group limit. A new streaming analytics team wants their own consumer group. How do you handle this at scale?
a: Standard tier is capped at 20 consumer groups per Event Hub; Premium and Dedicated tiers have higher limits (100 per hub on Premium). First audit the existing consumer groups — inactive ones from decommissioned applications should be deleted via PowerShell (`Remove-AzEventHubConsumerGroup`) to recover slots. If the limit is genuinely binding, upgrade the namespace to Premium or Dedicated via `Set-AzEventHubNamespace -SkuName Premium`. For large-scale multi-consumer architectures, a better pattern is to use a single dedicated consumer group per logical processing tier (e.g., one for real-time Azure Stream Analytics, one for batch Azure Event Hubs Capture) and fan out via a message bus or downstream topic rather than multiplying consumer groups. Alternatively, mirror the Event Hub to Azure Service Bus Topics, which support thousands of subscriptions.
fs: Audit + delete stale groups via PowerShell; upgrade to Premium for 100/hub; fan out via Service Bus Topics for massive fan-out.
fj: Creating a new Azure Event Hubs namespace per team — you lose ordered multi-consumer semantics, multiply costs, and fragment observability.

followups.q: How does upgrading an Azure Event Hubs namespace from Standard to Premium affect existing consumer groups and in-flight messages?
followups.a: Upgrading from Standard to Premium is an in-place operation that preserves existing Event Hubs, consumer groups, and all retained messages (up to the configured retention period). There's no data migration — the namespace is upgraded and reconnects clients. Consumer group checkpoint offsets stored in Azure Blob Storage or Cosmos DB remain valid. Test client reconnection behavior in staging first because Premium uses a different SKU endpoint and some older SDKs may require a configuration update.

q: Describe how you extend a Microsoft Purview business glossary with custom term templates and attribute inheritance so domain stewards can capture platform-specific metadata (e.g., Azure SQL Server column lineage tags vs. AKS service tags) without duplicating terms.
a: Purview term templates define the attribute schema for a category of terms. Create a base template ('Data Asset') with shared attributes (owner, classification, retention policy). Extend it with child templates using template inheritance: 'SQL Asset' adds Azure SQL Server-specific attributes (column lineage tag, query optimization hint, masking policy), while 'Compute Asset' adds AKS-specific attributes (service tag, namespace, cluster). Assign the appropriate template when a steward creates a term in the business glossary — the term inherits the base attributes plus its domain-specific ones. Link Azure SQL Server column assets to glossary terms via the Purview REST API to surface the lineage tag directly on column objects. For AKS service tags, register AKS-hosted microservice endpoints as custom source assets in Purview's data map and attach the Compute Asset template terms. This prevents duplicating the same logical concept (e.g., 'Customer ID') as separate SQL and AKS terms.
fs: Base template + child templates per platform (SQL Asset, Compute Asset) → inheritance avoids term duplication; REST API links column/service assets to terms.
fj: Creating separate duplicate business glossary terms for every platform variant — governance becomes inconsistent and the data catalog becomes a synonym maze.

followups.q: A compliance audit requires proof that every Azure SQL Server column marked PII in the business glossary has a column masking policy applied. How do you generate that report?
followups.a: Query the Purview REST API for all terms with the PII classification in the business glossary, then traverse the term-to-asset relationships to get the linked Azure SQL Server column assets. For each column, call the Azure SQL Server REST API or PowerShell `Get-AzSqlDatabaseDataMaskingRule` to check if a masking rule exists. Join the two result sets and emit a compliance gap report for any PII-tagged column without a masking rule. Automate this as a weekly Azure Automation runbook posting results to a Teams channel.

q: You are deploying Azure Event Hubs Capture to ADLS Gen2 for a near-real-time data lakehouse. How do you handle schema evolution when the Avro producer adds a new field mid-stream?
a: Register all schemas in the Azure Event Hubs Schema Registry before producers emit; use schema compatibility mode 'Backward' so consumers reading old Avro files can still deserialize new messages that have an additional nullable field. When a producer wants to add a field, it registers a new schema version — the registry enforces compatibility. Azure Event Hubs Capture writes Avro files with the schema embedded in the Avro container header, so downstream readers (e.g., Databricks `from_avro`) can extract the schema per file even if versions differ. Downstream, use Delta Lake schema evolution (`mergeSchema=true`) when writing Capture Avro files to a Delta table so the new column is auto-added to the table schema. Apply a business glossary term to the new column before promoting it to production in the Delta table, preserving governance continuity.
fs: Schema Registry Backward compat → Capture embeds schema in Avro header → Delta mergeSchema=true; link new column to business glossary term.
fj: Skipping Schema Registry and letting producers emit arbitrary Avro — field additions silently break deserializers and create unmarked columns in the Delta table with no business glossary linkage.

followups.q: Azure Event Hubs Capture writes small Avro files every minute. How do you compact them efficiently in Databricks without blocking real-time readers?
followups.a: Run a Databricks Auto Loader job that reads Capture Avro files and writes to a Delta table, then use Delta's `OPTIMIZE` command with `ZORDER BY` on query predicates on a scheduled cadence. The key is that Delta's copy-on-write doesn't block concurrent reads: readers see the pre-optimize snapshot until the optimize transaction commits. For streaming readers, ensure the checkpoint path is separate from the optimize target so Auto Loader offsets aren't disrupted.

q: You are using PowerShell to automate the deployment of a new Azure SQL Server logical server, elastic pool, and databases across dev/test/prod environments. How do you make the script idempotent and environment-aware?
a: Use an environment parameter (`-Environment dev|test|prod`) that maps to a configuration hashtable defining SKU, elastic pool DTUs/vCores, and firewall rules per environment. Wrap each resource creation with `Get-AzSqlServer`/`Get-AzSqlElasticPool`/`Get-AzSqlDatabase` — if the resource exists with the target configuration, skip the Set call; if it exists with drift, call `Set-Az*` to reconcile. Use `-WhatIf` and `-Confirm:$false` flags for dry-run and unattended modes. Store the script in source control and invoke it from an Azure DevOps pipeline with environment-specific variable groups rather than hardcoded secrets. Register the Azure SQL Server with Microsoft Purview's data map after creation using the Purview PowerShell module so the new server is immediately visible in the business glossary governance scope.
fs: Environment config hashtable; Get-then-Set idempotency; -WhatIf for dry-run; ADO pipeline with variable groups; register in Purview post-create.
fj: Using New-Az* without a Get guard — the script fails on re-run or partially duplicates resources, requiring manual cleanup.

followups.q: How do you manage Azure SQL Server firewall rules as code across environments using PowerShell without opening 0.0.0.0-255.255.255.255?
followups.a: Maintain a per-environment JSON file listing allowed IP CIDR ranges (corporate VPN exit IPs, Azure services CIDR via the 'Allow Azure services' flag). In the PowerShell script, call `Get-AzSqlServerFirewallRule` to get the current set, diff against the desired JSON, call `New-AzSqlServerFirewallRule` for missing rules and `Remove-AzSqlServerFirewallRule` for rules no longer in the desired set. Never use 0.0.0.0/0 even in dev — instead add the deployer's IP dynamically via `(Invoke-RestMethod ifconfig.me/ip)` and remove it after the deploy step completes.

q: Your AKS cluster hosts data pipeline pods that need to read from Azure SQL Server and write to ADLS Gen2. How do you manage credentials without any secrets in Kubernetes and enforce Query Optimization–aware connection pooling?
a: Use AKS Workload Identity: federate a Kubernetes service account to an Azure Managed Identity. Grant the managed identity 'db_datareader' on the Azure SQL Server database and 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' on ADLS Gen2. The pods authenticate via projected service account tokens exchanged for short-lived Entra ID tokens — no connection strings in Kubernetes Secrets. For connection pooling, use pyodbc/SqlAlchemy with a pool_size and max_overflow tuned to the Azure SQL Server's max_degree_of_parallelism and connection limit; use `pool_pre_ping=True` to discard stale connections silently. Add an Azure SQL Server connection-level hint (`OPTION (MAXDOP 4, RECOMPILE)`) for the ETL queries via Query Optimization hints in the ORM's execution options so the pod's query plan is tuned for the current data shape rather than a cached plan from a different parameter set.
fs: Workload Identity (no k8s secrets) → SqlAlchemy pool_pre_ping + tuned pool_size → per-query MAXDOP + RECOMPILE hints for Query Optimization.
fj: Storing the Azure SQL Server connection string in a Kubernetes Secret and relying on a single shared connection — secrets rotate, connections leak, and plan cache poisoning goes undetected.

followups.q: A Spark job on AKS is doing a large JOIN between a Delta Lake table and a dimension table in Azure SQL Server. How do you avoid a full table scan on SQL Server?
followups.a: Push the Spark broadcast join hint: `broadcast(spark.read.jdbc(sqlserver_url, dim_table))`. This reads the SQL Server dimension table once into Spark broadcast memory and eliminates per-partition SQL Server lookups. Ensure the JDBC read uses an indexed column for the predicate (`dbtable='(SELECT * FROM dim WHERE active=1) t'`) so SQL Server returns only active rows, and Query Optimization on SQL Server can use its covering index rather than scanning the full table. For recurring jobs, materialize the dimension as a Delta table and use Delta's optimized reads instead.

q: Explain how Lakehouse Federation in Unity Catalog differs from Azure Synapse Analytics external tables pointing to ADLS Gen2. When would you choose each?
a: Azure Synapse Analytics external tables (PolyBase/CETAS) define a schema over files on ADLS Gen2 — the data lives in the lake and Synapse reads it via a defined format (Parquet, CSV). These are file-based; there's no live pushdown to a remote OLTP or OLAP engine. Lakehouse Federation in Databricks Unity Catalog connects to live external query engines (Snowflake, Redshift, MySQL, SQL Server) via JDBC and pushes SQL predicates to those engines at runtime — the data stays in the remote system and is not copied to the lake. Choose Synapse external tables when the data is already on ADLS Gen2 and you want T-SQL analytics over lake files without a data move. Choose Lakehouse Federation when the authoritative source is a remote database and you want federated SQL queries alongside Delta Lake tables under unified Unity Catalog governance, without replicating the source data.
fs: Synapse external = file-on-lake with schema overlay; Lakehouse Federation = live JDBC pushdown to remote engine; choose Federation when source stays authoritative in the remote system.
fj: Treating them as equivalent alternatives — they're fundamentally different: one reads files, the other proxies a live engine.

followups.q: Can Lakehouse Federation in Unity Catalog write back to the foreign source, or is it read-only?
followups.a: As of early 2026, Lakehouse Federation is read-only — you can SELECT from foreign catalogs but not INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. For write-back scenarios, use the native JDBC write from Databricks (spark.write.jdbc) or ADF pipelines targeting the source system directly. Federation is designed for federated analytics and governance, not as a bi-directional sync layer.

q: You are building a business glossary migration from a legacy data catalog into Microsoft Purview. The legacy catalog has 2,000 terms, synonym relationships, and owner metadata. How do you automate the bulk import while preserving relationships?
a: Export the legacy catalog to JSON/CSV using its API. Map the legacy schema to Purview's Atlas term model: term name, longDescription, abbreviation, synonyms (array of term GUIDs), related terms, and contact (owner email mapped to Purview user objectId via Microsoft Graph API lookup). Write a PowerShell or Python script that: (1) creates all terms first without relationships to get GUIDs, (2) builds a name→GUID lookup map, (3) patches each term with synonym and related-term GUID arrays using the Purview REST API PATCH endpoint. Batch requests in groups of 100 to stay within API rate limits. For owner metadata, call `GET /users?$filter=mail eq '<email>'` on Microsoft Graph to resolve each owner email to an Entra ID objectId before calling Purview. Commit the final Purview-exported JSON to git as the new source of truth, completing the glossary-as-code pattern for the business glossary.
fs: Export → map to Atlas schema → bulk-create terms for GUIDs → patch synonym/related-term links; Graph API resolves owner emails; commit result to git.
fj: Manually re-creating 2,000 terms in the Purview portal — weeks of work, no auditability, no way to roll back.

followups.q: The Purview REST API returns 429 errors during bulk import. How do you handle rate limiting in the PowerShell migration script?
followups.a: Implement exponential backoff: catch 429 responses, read the `Retry-After` header, and sleep for that duration before retrying. Wrap the HTTP calls in a retry loop with jitter. Limit concurrency using PowerShell runspaces or a semaphore so you don't flood the endpoint — 3–5 concurrent threads is typically safe for Purview bulk writes. Log the failed term names to a retry queue file so a subsequent run can resume without re-processing successfully imported terms.

q: An AKS cluster running data pipelines needs to connect to an on-prem SQL Server database through Azure Private Link and an ExpressRoute circuit. Walk through the network path and how you validate connectivity from within a pod.
a: The path: AKS pod → AKS VNet → Private Endpoint in AKS VNet subnet → Private Link Service → ExpressRoute gateway → on-prem network → SQL Server. Configure a Private DNS Zone for `privatelink.database.windows.net` linked to the AKS VNet so pods resolve the SQL Server FQDN to the private IP of the private endpoint rather than the public IP. Grant the AKS system-assigned Managed Identity 'Network Contributor' on the Private Endpoint NIC's resource group if the endpoint was deployed separately. Validate from inside a pod: run a debug pod (`kubectl run -it --rm debug --image=mcr.microsoft.com/mssql-tools`) and use `sqlcmd -S <fqdn> -U <user> -P <pass> -Q 'SELECT 1'`. Use `nslookup <fqdn>` inside the pod to confirm it resolves to the private IP, not the public one. If DNS resolution fails, the Private DNS Zone is not linked to the pod's VNet or CoreDNS is not forwarding to the Azure DNS resolver (168.63.129.16).
fs: Pod → Private Endpoint → ExpressRoute → SQL Server; Private DNS Zone linked to AKS VNet; validate via sqlcmd + nslookup from debug pod.
fj: Pointing pods at the SQL Server public IP and relying on NSG allow rules — traffic traverses the internet and fails corporate data-residency policies.

followups.q: How do you prevent AKS pods from accidentally connecting to the public SQL Server endpoint even if the private endpoint DNS record is misconfigured?
followups.a: Apply a Kubernetes NetworkPolicy that allows egress from the pipeline pods only to the private endpoint IP CIDR and denies all other outbound except necessary Azure endpoints (Azure Monitor, ADLS Gen2 private endpoint). Additionally, use Azure Policy to deny the creation of Azure SQL Server public network access in the subscription ('Deny Public Network Access to Azure SQL Server') so the SQL Server can only be reached via private endpoint regardless of client configuration.

q: You need to implement Query Optimization for a high-concurrency Azure SQL Server workload serving 500 concurrent short OLTP queries while a background ETL job runs large scans. How do you isolate them?
a: Use Resource Governor (available on SQL Server on-prem and SQL Managed Instance) or, on Azure SQL Database, the Query Optimization approach via workload groups: classify OLTP sessions and ETL sessions into separate workload groups with capped CPU and memory percentages. On Azure SQL Database Hyperscale, use Named Replicas — route ETL reads to a named replica so the scan load never contends with the primary's OLTP execution. Enable Read-Committed Snapshot Isolation (RCSI) on the primary so OLTP readers are not blocked by ETL writers. Set `MAXDOP` per workload group: OLTP queries typically benefit from MAXDOP 1–2 (less thread overhead for short queries) while the ETL benefits from higher parallelism. Monitor via Query Store's per-query resource stats and `sys.dm_exec_requests` to confirm isolation is holding under load.
fs: Workload groups (Resource Governor on MI) or Named Replica for ETL reads; RCSI for reader/writer isolation; MAXDOP per group; validate via Query Store + dm_exec_requests.
fj: Running ETL and OLTP on the same SQL Server instance with default settings — ETL's full scans steal buffer pool pages, and table locks block OLTP readers even with snapshot isolation off.

followups.q: RCSI is enabled but OLTP queries are still being blocked by the ETL. What's the overlooked cause?
followups.a: RCSI eliminates shared-lock blocking by giving readers a row version, but DDL operations (ALTER TABLE, CREATE INDEX ONLINE) and schema stability locks still block under RCSI. If the ETL is rebuilding indexes online or updating statistics, those operations hold schema modification locks. Also check if the ETL is updating the same rows the OLTP is reading — RCSI ensures readers don't block writers, but writers still block each other. Use `sys.dm_os_wait_stats` and `sys.dm_exec_requests` to distinguish lock waits from latch waits.

q: Describe a Lakehouse Federation architecture using Databricks Unity Catalog that spans Azure SQL Server (operational), Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated pool (warehouse), and Delta Lake (lake). How do you enforce the business glossary as the single governance layer?
a: Register three connections in Unity Catalog: `sqlserver_ops` (SQL Server via JDBC), `synapse_dw` (Synapse dedicated pool via JDBC), and `delta_lake` (native metastore). Create foreign catalogs `ops_catalog` and `synapse_catalog` for the first two. Data scientists write SQL joining all three: `SELECT * FROM ops_catalog.dbo.orders JOIN synapse_catalog.dw.dim_customer USING (cust_id) JOIN main.delta.fact_events USING (cust_id)`. Unity Catalog enforces row filters and column masks uniformly across all three catalogs regardless of the underlying engine. Attach business glossary terms from Microsoft Purview to the Unity Catalog column assets via the Purview-Databricks integration (Unity Catalog exposes lineage to Purview's data map). This makes Purview the authoritative business glossary — every PII column across SQL Server, Synapse, and Delta Lake is tagged with the same term, masking policy, and owner, with lineage traced end-to-end.
fs: Three Unity Catalog connections → foreign catalogs → uniform RLS/masking in one SQL join; Purview business glossary linked to Unity Catalog column assets for end-to-end governance.
fj: Managing separate access control lists in SQL Server, Synapse, and Databricks independently — the same column has three different governance policies that drift over time.

followups.q: How do you handle stale statistics in the Azure SQL Server foreign catalog when Lakehouse Federation pushes a JOIN predicate to it?
followups.a: Lakehouse Federation generates and pushes SQL to SQL Server, which uses its own optimizer with its own statistics. If SQL Server statistics are stale, its optimizer may underestimate row counts and choose a nested-loop join where a hash join is better. Update statistics regularly on the SQL Server side (`UPDATE STATISTICS` scheduled via SQL Agent or Azure Automation PowerShell runbook) and enable auto-update statistics. On the Federation side, inspect the query plan using `EXPLAIN` to see how much data the RemoteScan returns — if it's unexpectedly large, the predicate pushdown may not be working and you may need to rewrite the query to push an explicit subquery to the foreign catalog.

q: An Azure Event Hubs–based IoT pipeline needs exactly-once semantics from ingestion through to a SQL Server sink for financial meter readings. How do you achieve this without a distributed transaction coordinator?
a: Azure Event Hubs guarantees at-least-once delivery (no native exactly-once). Achieve exactly-once semantics end-to-end with an idempotent write pattern on the SQL Server sink: include the Event Hubs partition ID and sequence number as composite natural keys in the target table and use `MERGE` (upsert) keyed on those columns. If a consumer crashes and replays from checkpoint, the `MERGE` on SQL Server silently ignores duplicate sequence numbers instead of inserting duplicates. Store the consumer checkpoint (offset) in the same SQL Server database inside a transaction with the data write so the checkpoint and the data row advance atomically — never checkpoint before the write or after a failed write. Use the latest Azure Event Hubs SDK (EventProcessorClient) which stores checkpoints externally (Azure Blob or SQL Server) and resumes from the committed offset. Validate idempotency under failure injection using a chaos testing run in your AKS test environment.
fs: Idempotent MERGE on SQL Server keyed on partition+sequence; checkpoint and data write in same DB transaction; EventProcessorClient for offset management.
fj: Treating Azure Event Hubs as exactly-once out of the box and skipping deduplication on SQL Server — financial meter duplicates slip through during consumer restarts.

code.label: Idempotent MERGE into SQL Server sink
lines: AS src (partition_id, seq_no, meter_id, reading_kwh, ts)

followups.q: The SQL Server MERGE table is growing huge with partition+sequence deduplication keys. How do you manage its size without losing exactly-once guarantees?
followups.a: Partition the deduplication table by `ts` (the event timestamp) and use a sliding-window partition switch to archive old partitions. Events replayed after the archive window would be re-inserted, so tune the window to be longer than your maximum consumer lag plus your recovery SLA (e.g., retain 7 days of dedup keys). For long-term dedup beyond 7 days, accept that late-arriving duplicates are statistically improbable given your checkpoint interval and document the bounded-window guarantee in the SLA.

q: You are using Databricks Unity Catalog Lakehouse Federation to serve analysts who query both Azure SQL Server operational tables and Delta Lake fact tables. How do you handle Query Optimization when the pushed-down SQL to SQL Server is generating inefficient plans?
a: Lakehouse Federation generates SQL and pushes it to Azure SQL Server via JDBC. Inspect the effective SQL using `EXPLAIN` on the Databricks side and `sys.dm_exec_query_stats` + `sys.dm_exec_sql_text` on the SQL Server side to see the arriving query. Common issues: (1) Lakehouse Federation may not push complex predicates containing Spark functions — rewrite the query to use SQL Server–compatible expressions so predicate pushdown fires. (2) SQL Server statistics may be stale for columns used in federation JOINs — run `UPDATE STATISTICS` via a PowerShell Azure Automation runbook on a weekly schedule. (3) If the SQL Server table lacks an index on the JOIN key, the pushed query causes a full table scan — add a covering index for the federation access pattern. Monitor bytes transferred from SQL Server to Databricks in Spark UI (JDBC shuffle read) as the key Query Optimization signal: high bytes means pushdown isn't filtering enough at source.
fs: EXPLAIN + dm_exec_query_stats to inspect pushed SQL; ensure predicate is SQL Server-compatible; covering index on JOIN key; watch JDBC bytes in Spark UI.
fj: Assuming Lakehouse Federation always pushes all predicates — complex Spark functions are not translated and cause full SQL Server table scans that look like Databricks slowness.

followups.q: How do you cache frequently-joined SQL Server dimension tables when accessed via Lakehouse Federation to reduce JDBC overhead?
followups.a: Read the SQL Server dimension table via Lakehouse Federation once per job and cache it in Spark: `dim_df = spark.table('ops_catalog.dbo.dim_customer').cache()`. For hourly jobs, persist the cached DataFrame across multiple pipeline stages. For daily-refresh dimensions, materialize as a Delta table in the lake using a scheduled Databricks job and point Federation-based queries at the Delta copy for the hot read path, reserving the live Lakehouse Federation for freshness checks.

q: Your Lakehouse Federation foreign catalog in Unity Catalog points to an Azure SQL Server database. A business glossary review finds that three columns exposed via the foreign catalog lack classification tags. How do you apply column-level tags to foreign catalog columns in Unity Catalog?
a: Unity Catalog supports tagging foreign catalog columns with the same `ALTER TABLE … SET TAGS` DDL used for native Delta tables. The tags are stored in Unity Catalog's metastore, not pushed to Azure SQL Server — the physical SQL Server schema is unchanged. Steps: (1) Identify the columns via `SHOW COLUMNS IN ops_catalog.dbo.customer_pii`. (2) Apply classification tags: `ALTER TABLE ops_catalog.dbo.customer_pii ALTER COLUMN email SET TAGS ('pii' = 'true', 'glossary_term' = 'Customer Email')`. (3) Run a Unity Catalog lineage query to confirm the tag propagates to any Delta Lake downstream table derived from this foreign column. Sync the tag set with the Microsoft Purview business glossary term 'Customer Email' via the Purview-Unity Catalog connector so the term appears in both catalogs. This means the business glossary is the single vocabulary regardless of whether data lives in SQL Server, Synapse, or Delta Lake.
fs: ALTER TABLE … ALTER COLUMN SET TAGS on foreign catalog columns; tags live in Unity Catalog, not SQL Server; sync to Purview business glossary for unified vocabulary.
fj: Tagging only Delta Lake tables in Unity Catalog and leaving foreign catalog columns untagged — creates governance blind spots where SQL Server columns appear unclassified in lineage graphs.

followups.q: Does tagging a foreign catalog column in Unity Catalog cause Unity Catalog to enforce column masking on that column for SQL Server reads?
followups.a: Yes — Unity Catalog column masks are enforced at the query layer in Databricks regardless of the physical storage. A mask on `ops_catalog.dbo.customer_pii.email` will obfuscate the value returned to any non-privileged user running a Lakehouse Federation query, even though the underlying SQL Server column is unmasked. The masking expression runs in Databricks before the result is returned, not in SQL Server — so SQL Server-native dynamic data masking and Unity Catalog masking are independent layers.

q: How do you monitor Lakehouse Federation query performance and data transfer costs in Azure when the foreign source is SQL Server and the compute is AKS-hosted Databricks?
a: Instrument at three layers: (1) Spark UI on Databricks — inspect the 'SQL' tab for JDBC scan metrics (bytes read from SQL Server, rows returned); high bytes indicates missing predicate pushdown. (2) Azure SQL Server — enable Query Store and set up Azure Monitor metrics on the SQL Server logical server (DTU/vCore %, connection count, deadlocks); use PowerShell `Get-AzMetric` to pull these into a monitoring script. (3) AKS networking — for AKS-hosted Databricks workers, monitor egress bytes from the AKS VNet via Azure Network Watcher flow logs and ensure JDBC traffic is routing through the private endpoint rather than the public SQL Server endpoint. Combine these signals in an Azure Monitor workbook that alerts when JDBC bytes transferred per federation query exceeds a threshold, indicating a full-table-scan pattern that needs Query Optimization attention on the SQL Server side.
fs: Spark UI JDBC metrics + SQL Server Query Store + AKS Network Watcher egress; Azure Monitor workbook ties all three; alert on JDBC bytes > threshold.
fj: Monitoring only Databricks job duration — missing that a slow job is caused by a full SQL Server scan over the AKS private endpoint that could be fixed with an index.

followups.q: An AKS-hosted Databricks worker is making thousands of small JDBC calls to SQL Server via Lakehouse Federation instead of one batched read. What's the cause and fix?
followups.a: This is the N+1 JDBC problem: the Databricks query plan has a nested-loop that calls SQL Server once per partition or per row instead of a bulk scan. Root cause is usually a subquery or UDF inside the federation join that prevents Spark from batching the read. Fix: rewrite to eliminate the per-row lookup, use a broadcast join for small SQL Server dimensions, or materialize the SQL Server table as a Delta snapshot and join against the snapshot rather than making live JDBC calls.

q: You need to use Lakehouse Federation in Unity Catalog to expose an Azure SQL Server table to analysts but the SQL Server is in a private VNet with no public endpoint. How do you configure the network path and the Unity Catalog connection credential?
a: Lakehouse Federation JDBC connections from Databricks to SQL Server must traverse a network path. Options: (1) If Databricks is deployed in the same VNet as the SQL Server private endpoint (VNet injection), JDBC traffic stays within Azure backbone — configure the Unity Catalog connection with the SQL Server private FQDN (e.g., `sqlserver-prod.privatelink.database.windows.net`). (2) If Databricks is in a separate VNet, use VNet peering between the Databricks VNet and the SQL Server VNet, ensuring the Databricks cluster nodes can resolve the Private DNS Zone for `privatelink.database.windows.net`. For credentials, use a Unity Catalog service credential backed by an Azure Managed Identity that has `db_datareader` on the SQL Server database — no passwords stored in Unity Catalog. Validate with `TEST CONNECTION` in the Unity Catalog connection UI before creating the foreign catalog. Run a PowerShell network test from an AKS debug pod (`Test-NetConnection -ComputerName <fqdn> -Port 1433`) to confirm Layer-4 connectivity before configuring Lakehouse Federation.
fs: VNet injection or peering for JDBC path; Private DNS Zone for privatelink FQDN; Managed Identity credential in Unity Catalog; validate with PowerShell Test-NetConnection from AKS.
fj: Enabling the SQL Server public network endpoint just to make Lakehouse Federation work — exposes the database to internet traffic and violates private-connectivity requirements.

followups.q: After configuring Lakehouse Federation over a private endpoint, analysts see sporadic connection timeouts. What's the most likely cause in a VNet-injected Databricks cluster?
followups.a: The most common cause is DNS resolution falling back to the public SQL Server IP when the Private DNS Zone is not linked to the Databricks VNet. The first query resolves correctly (cached), then a DNS TTL expiry causes a re-lookup that returns the public IP, which is blocked by NSG — causing a timeout. Link the `privatelink.database.windows.net` Private DNS Zone to the Databricks delegated subnet VNet and enable auto-registration. Also check that the Databricks cluster's NSG allows outbound port 1433 to the private endpoint IP only.

q: A data governance audit requires you to demonstrate end-to-end lineage from an Azure Event Hubs ingest stream through Delta Lake transformations to a Power BI report, with each column linked to a Purview business glossary term. How do you instrument this in a Databricks + Purview architecture?
a: Enable Unity Catalog lineage tracking in Databricks — every `spark.read`/`spark.write` and SQL operation is automatically captured in the Unity Catalog lineage graph. Register the Azure Event Hubs Capture ADLS Gen2 path as a Unity Catalog external location so Spark reads from it via Unity Catalog and lineage is captured from source. For Microsoft Purview integration, enable the Databricks Unity Catalog Purview connector (Azure Marketplace managed app) which scans Unity Catalog metadata and pushes column-level lineage to the Purview data map. In the Purview data map, link each column asset to the appropriate business glossary term. On the Power BI side, connect the semantic model to the Databricks Delta Lake via the Azure Databricks connector and run a Purview Power BI scan so the report columns appear in the Purview lineage graph, connected back through Delta to the Azure Event Hubs source. The business glossary term now spans the full chain: Event Hubs ingest → Delta transform → Power BI report column.
fs: Unity Catalog auto-lineage → Purview connector pushes to Purview data map → business glossary terms linked to columns → Power BI scan closes the lineage loop.
fj: Manually documenting lineage in a spreadsheet and linking glossary terms ad-hoc — lineage goes stale the moment a pipeline changes.

followups.q: The Purview scan of Databricks Unity Catalog runs nightly but lineage is 24 hours stale for compliance queries. How do you get near-real-time lineage?
followups.a: Enable Unity Catalog's OpenLineage API integration — this emits lineage events to a compatible endpoint (Marquez, OpenLineage-compatible Purview HTTP connector) in near-real-time as jobs complete, rather than waiting for a batch scan. Alternatively, configure a Databricks job post-hook that triggers the Purview Unity Catalog scan API endpoint on completion of high-value pipelines. For Azure Event Hubs–based pipelines, trigger the Purview scan via an Event Grid subscription on the ADLS Gen2 path where Capture writes Avro files.

q: Describe the steps to decommission a Lakehouse Federation foreign catalog in Unity Catalog when the source Azure SQL Server is being retired, without breaking downstream Delta pipelines that still reference some of the same tables.
a: First, run Unity Catalog lineage queries to enumerate every notebook, job, and Delta table that reads from the foreign catalog: `SELECT * FROM system.access.audit WHERE object_type='TABLE' AND object_name LIKE 'ops_catalog.%'`. Identify which foreign tables are referenced in Delta pipelines. For each referenced table, materialize the last snapshot as a native Delta table (`CREATE TABLE main.ops_archive.customer AS SELECT * FROM ops_catalog.dbo.customer`) so downstream pipelines can redirect to the Delta copy. Update all pipeline definitions (notebooks, DLT pipelines, SQL queries) to use the archive Delta tables. After all consumers are redirected, use `DROP FOREIGN CATALOG ops_catalog CASCADE` in Unity Catalog — this removes all foreign table metadata without touching SQL Server. Coordinate the SQL Server retirement date with the business glossary term owner in Microsoft Purview so the terms are updated to reflect the new authoritative source (Delta Lake archive). Run a PowerShell script to remove the SQL Server's Entra ID service principal permissions now that the catalog is dropped.
fs: Audit lineage → materialize referenced tables to Delta → redirect consumers → DROP FOREIGN CATALOG CASCADE → update Purview business glossary owner/source; revoke SP via PowerShell.
fj: Dropping the foreign catalog before redirecting consumers — breaks all downstream Delta pipelines instantly with 'catalog not found' errors.

followups.q: How do you validate that no pipelines are still hitting the foreign catalog after you've redirected all consumers?
followups.a: Query `system.access.audit` in Unity Catalog for any `SELECT` or `TABLE_ACCESS` events on the foreign catalog tables for the 7 days following the redirect cutover. Set up a Unity Catalog tag on the old foreign catalog as 'deprecated' and configure a Databricks alert that emails the steward if any access event occurs. Only proceed to `DROP FOREIGN CATALOG` once the audit log shows zero access for the retention window.

q: How do you implement column-level lineage in Microsoft Purview for data that flows from Azure Event Hubs Capture through Databricks Delta Lake transformations, with every column linked to a Lakehouse Federation–registered business glossary term?
a: Enable Unity Catalog column-level lineage in Databricks (requires Unity Catalog and SQL warehouses or DBR 12+). Each Spark SQL transformation records input→output column mappings automatically. The Purview–Databricks Unity Catalog connector (pushed via the Azure Purview managed app) syncs this lineage graph into Purview's data map, including column-level edges. For Azure Event Hubs Capture files (Avro on ADLS Gen2), register the ADLS Gen2 path as a Unity Catalog external location — Databricks Auto Loader reads from it and Unity Catalog captures the lineage edge from the Avro file path to the target Delta table column. In Purview, attach each column asset in the lineage graph to its business glossary term using the REST API term-assignment endpoint. For columns accessed via Lakehouse Federation foreign catalogs (e.g., a SQL Server dimension joined in a Databricks pipeline), Unity Catalog also records those foreign column inputs in the lineage graph, so the Purview data map shows a complete chain: Azure Event Hubs → ADLS Avro → Delta column → Lakehouse Federation SQL Server dimension column → business glossary term. This gives auditors a single Purview lineage view covering all three data sources under one business glossary.
fs: Unity Catalog auto column lineage → Purview connector → ADLS Gen2 external location for Event Hubs Capture → Lakehouse Federation foreign columns in lineage; all columns linked to Purview business glossary terms.
fj: Relying on table-level lineage only — column-level gaps mean you cannot prove which specific Azure Event Hubs field maps to the 'Customer Revenue' business glossary term in the final Power BI report.

followups.q: A Purview data steward notices that a business glossary term 'Customer Revenue' is linked to 15 columns across SQL Server, Delta Lake, and the Lakehouse Federation foreign catalog but only 3 have column masking policies. How do you close the gap systematically?
followups.a: Query Purview REST API for all column assets assigned the 'Customer Revenue' business glossary term. For each asset, determine the physical store type (SQL Server → check `sys.extended_properties` for masking; Delta Lake → check Unity Catalog column masks). Use PowerShell (`Get-AzSqlDatabaseDataMaskingRule`) for SQL Server columns and Unity Catalog's `SHOW COLUMN MASKS` for Delta. Generate a gap report of columns without masking and apply masks via the appropriate API: `ALTER TABLE … ALTER COLUMN SET MASK` in Unity Catalog (applies to Lakehouse Federation foreign catalog columns too) and `New-AzSqlDatabaseDataMaskingRule` in PowerShell for SQL Server.
